How do physical systems achieve collective intelligence and self-repair without a central brain?

A new paper published in Nature Communications from Sakana AI, IT University of Copenhagen, Autodesk, presents a beautiful realization of biologically inspired robotics: Smart Cellular Bricks.

Thread 🧵

I can't remember the last time I wasn't excited by new research Sakana put out. Easily one of the most creative labs out there. Collective intelligence is an interesting domain to begin with, but even within that domain their work always seems to be extra fascinating.

We are pleased to share our latest research, now published in Nature Communications: “Smart Cellular Bricks: Physical Modules That Recognize Their Own Shape and Repair Themselves.”

Blog: sakana.ai/smart-cellul...
Paper: www.nature.com/articles/s41...

Thread 🧵

reply_context.text: Wait, actually that's not all. It's also possible to reject (discrete, symbolic) representations, without rejecting (structural, relational, functional) representation.

posts.text: absolutely. representational spaces are continuous manifolds. the "discretization" is a neighborhood rather than a true bin. this is why we don't actually think in terms of prototypes. "chair" is not a discrete thing in my representational space. it doesn't even have a well defined boundary.

reply_context.text: It is possible to reject (discrete, digital, algorithmic) computationalism without rejecting the (much-needed) concept of representations. That is all. As you were.
